stories.title: Inland Real Estate Group Offloads Lakewood Apartment Community for $71.5M
stories.excerpt: Inland Real Estate Group sold Westlink at Oak Station at 1665 Pierson St. in Lakewood for $71.5 million. Inland bought the property in 2016 for $63 million. Amenities at the community include a pool and hot tub, fitness and business centers, and a clubhouse. The acquisition was facilitated by CBRE’s Denver Multifamily team, which was led by Shane Ozment and Terrance Hunt. Debt financing was arranged by CBRE’s Debt and Structured Finance group led by Ryan Greer and Troy Tegeler. For nearly six decades, Inland’s dozen member companies have supported investment, development, and finance products through an end-to-end suite of commercial real estate services spanning acquisitions, asset management, property management, and operations. The post Inland Real Estate Group Offloads Lakewood Apartment Community for $71.5M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Inland Real Estate sold Lakewood apartment (Westlink at Oak Station) for $71.5M, gaining $8.5M on 2016 purchase.
Denver multifamily trading at stabilized cap rates; modest appreciation over 8 years signals mature, slower-growth market.
CBRE debt placement indicates capital still accessible for quality Denver-area assets despite broader rate environment.
stories.summary: Inland Real Estate offloaded a Lakewood, Colorado apartment community (Westlink at Oak Station, 1665 Pierson St.) for $71.5 million, an $8.5 million gain on its $63 million 2016 acquisition. CBRE's Denver multifamily team facilitated the sale, with debt financing arranged by CBRE's debt group. The property features standard luxury amenities (pool, fitness center, clubhouse).
stories.impact: Limited. This transaction reflects a mature, stabilized Denver market with modest appreciation potential—roughly 1.3% annualized returns over 8 years. For developers in Denver metro, signal is cautious: rent growth is modest, cap rates are compressed, and acquisition timing is less about growth tailwinds and more about finding operational efficiencies or niche submarkets. Development feasibility in Denver proper remains tight; focus should shift to secondary suburbs with population migration or job anchors.

stories.title: Blake Investment wins bid for $8B Tropicana Field redevelopment
stories.excerpt: A team led by Blake Investment Partners won a bid to redevelop St. Petersburg’s Historic Gas Plant District, home to Tropicana Field and one of the most sought-after development opportunities in the Southeastern United States. St. Petersburg Mayor Ken Welch selected Blake Investment Partners, led by St. Petersburg native Thompson Blake, to redevelop the 86-acre site, the Tampa Bay Times reported. Blake’s team includes the Related Group and hedge fund Elliott Investment Management. The firm beat out the competing finalist with “The Burg Bid,” a proposal to purchase 58 acres for $275 million and pour $8.1 billion into the project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blake Investment, Related Group, Elliott Management selected for $8.1B Tropicana Field redevelopment; $275M land purchase for 86 acres.
Plan includes 3,600 income-restricted units (rental + for-sale) alongside hotels, museums, parks; 20-year phased delivery.
Tampa Bay metro gains major mixed-use anchor; supply pipeline reset after Hurricane Milton repairs and prior bid failures.
stories.summary: Blake Investment Partners, led by St. Petersburg native Thompson Blake and partnered with Related Group and Elliott Investment Management, won the bid to redevelop the Historic Gas Plant District and Tropicana Field site in St. Petersburg. The team will purchase 58 acres for $275 million and invest $8.1 billion total. The plan includes 3,600 income-restricted housing units (rental and for-sale), a new home for the Woodson African American Museum, parks, hotels, and cultural anchors, with execution expected over two decades. The Tampa Bay Rays stadium will be demolished and replaced.
stories.impact: Significant demand and capital signal for Tampa Bay metro. The 3,600 units—heavily income-restricted—will anchor long-term rental demand in St. Petersburg's urban core and likely draw younger, service-sector workers. The 20-year timeline and $8.1B capital commitment signal institutional confidence in the market's recovery post-Milton and demonstrate that major trophy sites can still attract marquee developers and co-investors. For competitors, this locks up supply in the prime submarket; secondary Tampa submarkets (Clearwater, Brooksville corridors) become relatively more attractive for faster-cycle development. Monitor affordability requirements—they may compress rents in competitive segments.

stories.title: Stevenson affiliate cuts unit count at proposed Glendale multifamily project
stories.excerpt: A developer is downsizing its proposed multifamily development in Glendale more than two years after the plans first surfaced.  A new application filed with the City of Glendale outlines new plans from 550 Riverdale LLC, the owner of the development site at 2748 San Fernando Road in the city’s Pacific-Edison neighborhood, Urbanize Los Angeles reported. The LLC is linked in state business records to David Stevenson, president of Glendale-based Stevenson Real Estate Services, and the real estate firm’s Glendale address. In 2024, the Robert and Stefanie Stevenson Family Trust — the property owner at the time — filed an application […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Stevenson affiliate scaled back Glendale project from 249 units to 229 units; still pursuing 7-story entitlement in 6-story zone.
Developer offering 27 affordable units (11.8% of total) via density bonus incentive to unlock additional floor area.
Glendale has 3 years to deliver 13,425 new units; pipeline remains tight despite local demand signals.
stories.summary: A Stevenson-affiliated LLC (550 Riverdale LLC) filed a revised application for its multifamily project at 2748 San Fernando Road in Glendale, downsizing from 249 units to 229 units in a seven-story building. The developer is seeking density bonus incentives to exceed the zone's six-story height limit and will provide 27 affordable rental units in exchange. The project sits on a 247,000-square-foot site and includes 281 parking spaces.
stories.impact: Marginal supply signal in a constrained market. Glendale's state mandate to deliver 13,425 units by 2029 leaves only 3 years of runway; 229 units is a modest contribution. The unit reduction and reliance on density bonus (affordability trade-off) suggests developer caution on construction costs, financing terms, or absorption risk—typical when market sentiment softens or entitlement risk rises. For competitors: Glendale zoning constraints and affordability requirements will remain friction points; focus on sites with existing entitlements or lower affordability thresholds. The affordability requirement (11.8% set-aside) erodes pro-forma yields and may tighten overall project economics in this submarket.

stories.title: LCOR lands $193M construction loan for Edgewater tower amid flurry of apartment projects
stories.excerpt: LCOR scored a $192.5 million construction loan for a 544-unit luxury apartment tower in Edgewater, as developers are excitedly pursuing multifamily projects across South Florida, despite a lingering oversupply.  New York-based LCOR started construction of a 39-story building at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard in Miami, according to the developer’s news release. The 1.1-acre site is a block from waterfront and Margaret Pace Park. The construction loan amounts to $354,000 per unit. Designed by ODP Architect, with interiors by Asprea Studio, the tower will include more than 40,000 square feet of amenities, over 10,000 square feet of ground-floor retail and a 448-space […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: LCOR secured $192.5M construction loan (Edgewater, Miami) at $354K per unit; Natixis lead on consortium financing.
544-unit luxury tower at 1775 Biscayne; completion Q4 2028. Lender confidence despite South Florida oversupply (18,600 units delivered 2024).
Floating-rate construction debt accessible for trophy Miami assets; underlying demand softness unresolved.
stories.summary: LCOR secured a $192.5 million construction loan for a 39-story, 544-unit luxury apartment tower in Miami's Edgewater neighborhood (1775 Biscayne Boulevard). The loan, arranged by Natixis Corporate and Investment Banking with co-lenders including Wells Fargo and others, represents $354,000 per unit and matures in 2029. The tower will deliver in Q4 2028 and features studios through three-bedroom units, 29 penthouses, 40,000 SF of amenities, 10,000 SF of ground-floor retail, and a 448-space garage. LCOR acquired the 1.1-acre site in 2022 for $49 million.
stories.impact: Capital availability signal masking demand weakness. Lenders remain willing to fund trophy assets in Miami's waterfront submarkets, but 18,600 units delivered in 2024—outpacing leasing by 20%—signals structural oversupply. South Florida's rental market is experiencing downward rent pressure; new supply (especially luxury) will face extended lease-up and muted pricing power. For developers: construction financing flows to marquee locations and sponsors with balance sheets (LCOR, Related, Ashton Woods tier), but underwriting standards for secondary assets remain tight. Avoid competing directly in the Edgewater/Wynwood/Midtown supply glut; focus on secondary markets (Broward, Palm Beach County, Tampa Bay) where supply pipelines are lighter and rent growth is more plausible. The $354K per unit financing suggests modest yield expansion over acquisition cost—a sign lenders are pricing risk appropriately, not greed.

stories.title: David Martin in talks to join Related, BH on Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment
stories.excerpt: Developer David Martin is in talks to join the Related Group and BH Group on their planned redevelopment of the Hollywood Beach Resort, sources told The Real Deal.  The Pérez family’s Coconut Grove-based Related and Isaac and Liat Toledano’s Aventura-based BH Group are under contract to acquire the 4.5-acre property at 101 North Ocean Drive, as previously reported by TRD. The Chetrit Group is selling the century-old resort. A source said the property is under contract for $100 million.  Martin, CEO of Terra Group declined to comment through a spokesperson. In a statement, BH named Terra and Related as its […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terra Group, Related, BH Group partnering on Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment; $100M acquisition of 4.5-acre oceanfront property.
Chetrit Group seller; 398-unit legacy resort asset being repositioned. Vested development rights challenged at city level.
Terra/Related also co-developing luxury condo at 2085 North Flagler Drive, West Palm Beach ($100M land for 1.6 acres).
stories.summary: David Martin's Terra Group is in talks to join Related Group and BH Group on the redevelopment of the Hollywood Beach Resort, a century-old oceanfront property on 4.5 acres at 101 North Ocean Drive. Related and BH are under contract to acquire the asset from the Chetrit Group for $100 million. The site contains 398 legacy resort units and faces local permitting friction (city commissioner voted against affirming vested development rights). Terra and BH are also working together on a luxury condo deal at 2085 North Flagler Drive in West Palm Beach, with a $100 million land purchase on 1.6 acres.
stories.impact: Capital and regulatory risk signal for South Florida beachfront repositioning. The Chetrit sale at $100M reflects distressed motivation (lender foreclosure filings noted); Related and BH's ability to absorb the deal and fund redevelopment signals institutional confidence, but the city commissioner's opposition to vested rights affirms local anti-density sentiment increasingly present in Miami-Dade. Beachfront legacy hospitality-to-residential conversions face longer entitlement cycles and affordability pressure. For developers targeting South Florida waterfront: expect 18–24 month permitting windows, design-review friction, and community opposition; structure partnerships with local operators (Terra Group) to navigate politics. The parallel West Palm Beach condo deal ($100M for 1.6 acres ≈ $62.5M/acre) suggests stronger underlying land economics north of Miami proper—a secondary-market signal worth monitoring.

stories.title: Whirlybird dreams: Ken Griffin wants helipad for Miami Beach megayacht marina
stories.excerpt: Billionaire Citadel founder Ken Griffin has already conquered the land and the sea in Miami Beach. His next target? The air.  City commissioners approved Griffin’s massive private megayacht marina at 120 MacArthur Causeway a few months ago, but now Griffin wants to chopper in. He submitted a request for a private, waterfront helipad at his sprawling Terminal Island development, the Miami Herald reported. The proposal, sponsored by Miami Beach Commissioner Joseph Magazine, is navigating the city’s Land Use and Sustainability Committee and the Planning Board. To clear the runway for Griffin, officials would need to amend Miami Beach’s land development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Ken Griffin's Terminal Island megayacht marina (Miami Beach, 3.5-acre industrial site) approved; now pursuing private helipad amendment.
Griffin acquired 3.5-acre waterfront site from Related Group; assemble Star Island parcels for $169M; owns $175M superyacht.
Luxury amenity creep (helipad entitlement request) signals ultra-high-net-worth real estate demand in Miami Beach; non-multifamily context.
stories.summary: Ken Griffin, billionaire Citadel founder, received Miami Beach city commission approval for a private megayacht marina at Terminal Island (120 MacArthur Causeway, 3.5 acres). He is now seeking approval for a private helipad to complement the facility. The marina will include eight megayacht slips (four-vessel cap), owner's pavilion, crew facilities, and rooftop space. Griffin acquired the site from Related Group and has spent $169 million assembling residential parcels on nearby Star Island.
stories.impact: Negligible for multifamily development. This is a niche ultra-luxury maritime/aerospace use case that does not meaningfully affect residential supply, demand, or financing. However, the indirect signal is relevant: Griffin's $169M Star Island assemblage and willingness to fund specialized amenities (marina, helipad) indicate persistent UHNW capital inflow to Miami Beach's barrier islands. This capital may compete for scarce land and drive site acquisition costs higher in Miami Beach proper, squeezing multifamily pro formas in adjacent neighborhoods. For developers: Miami Beach's multifamily pipeline should focus on mid-to-upper-market rental (not ultra-luxury condo, which competes with UHNW investment real estate) and secondarily on Wynwood, Midtown, and Edgewater where land basis is lower and rental demand is broader.

stories.title: Houston’s office glut is fueling a demolition trend for mostly vacant properties
stories.excerpt: As the Houston office market struggles to recover at the same rate as other similar cities, sometimes just tearing the buildings down is better for everyone involved.  Record low office vacancies and a need for private demolition has led to more demand than capital available for demolition work, according to Bisnow. Houston had an office vacancy rate of 26.8 percent, according to the outlet, a staggering figure attributable to old or obsolete office buildings and the pandemic ripple effect on employee hybrid schedules.  Vacant or near-vacant buildings all over the city can’t be demolished due to debt and lenders that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Houston office vacancy hits 26.8%; demolition demand outstrips contractor capacity. Grant Mackay reporting supply-constrained demo market.
Obsolete office stock and pandemic hybrid shift driving structural emptiness. Some lenders blocking dispositions to avoid ownership.
Dallas and other Texas metros show faster office recovery; Houston negative absorption persists despite 2025 sales uptick.
stories.summary: Houston's office market faces a historic 26.8% vacancy rate, driving elevated demolition demand that has outstripped contractors' capacity. Older, obsolete buildings and pandemic-era hybrid-work adoption have left many properties vacant or near-vacant. Some lenders are blocking demolitions to avoid taking ownership, trapping capital. Grant Mackay Demolition Company reports demand far exceeding capability. While Dallas and other Texas metros have recovered more quickly, Houston's office absorption remains negative despite sales activity uptick in 2025, much of which involved distressed or conversion deals.
stories.impact: Houston office-to-multifamily conversion opportunity and cautionary tale. The 26.8% office vacancy and lender blocking create a pipeline lag for adaptive reuse projects—developers pursuing office-to-residential conversions will face financing friction and extended timelines as lenders unwind non-performing office debt. However, the *structural* nature of Houston's office oversupply (obsolescence, not cyclical vacancy) means ground-floor retail and amenity space in converted towers will remain challenging to lease. For Houston multifamily developers: avoid over-reliance on conversion deals; focus on greenfield development in supply-constrained submarkets (Uptown, Midtown near employment anchors) or secondary metros (Austin, San Antonio, Dallas suburbs) where office conversion risk is lower and land costs are rising. The demolition bottleneck also signals that land supply in some Houston neighborhoods may *tighten* as vacant buildings are razed—creating speculative acquisition opportunity for patient capital.

stories.title: Inflationary Concerns Send School Shoppers Back to Discount Stores: Report
stories.excerpt: Parents’ back-to-school budgets are up nearly 12 percent this year, according to JLL’s 2026 Back-to-School Survey, and that’s good news for discount stores. The report, released Thursday, saw the dollar store sector enter the survey’s top 10 list of parents’ most-cited shopping destinations. The sector’s rising star comes as Americans continue to tighten belts in the face of inflation. The brokerage’s online survey collected responses from 1,022 U.S. parents with children between the ages of 5 and 18. Its release coincides with the July back-to-school shopping surge, when 27 percent of surveyed parents plan to check off their children’s classroom wish lists. Over 90 percent of parents plan to shop at physical stores this year, according to the survey, and dollar stores and mass merchants dominated their spending plans. The popularity of cheaper retailers such as Dollar Tree and Walmart are no surprise –– the report found school shopping budgets rising 11.7 percent from last year, up to an average of $489 per child. The uptick runs well ahead of the current U.S. inflation rate of around 4 percent. “Families are seeking retailers that respect their time and budgets, which explains why 68.7 percent cite saving money, and 35.1 percent prioritize finding the full school list in one place,” James Cook, JLL’s Americas director of retail research, said in a statement. The largest budget hike was found among middle-income families, who raised their spending expectations roughly 20 percent this year to $495 per child. The number of discretionary items on parents’ shopping lists fell in kind for the second consecutive year, meaning fewer fidget spinners and novelty pens will be landing in shopping carts. Consumers are shopping more strategically, the report said, with stores like Dollar General and Dollar Tree gaining ground for their discounts, and mass retailers like Walmart taking the top spot for their one-stop-shop convenience. Walmart popularity r
bullets: Parents' back-to-school spending up 11.7% YoY to $489/child; dollar stores entering JLL survey's top 10 shopping destinations.
90%+ of parents plan in-store shopping; discount retailers (Dollar Tree, Walmart) gaining share amid inflation concerns.
Consumer budget pressure evident; retail sector consolidating toward value/discount formats over full-price.
stories.summary: JLL's 2026 Back-to-School Survey found that U.S. parents' shopping budgets rose 11.7% year-over-year to an average of $489 per child, even as inflation concerns drive spending toward discount retailers. Dollar stores have entered the survey's top 10 list of shopping destinations for the first time. Over 90% of parents plan to shop at physical stores, with mass merchants and dollar stores dominating their spending plans. July represents peak back-to-school shopping season, with 27% of surveyed parents planning major purchases during that window.
stories.impact: Minimal for multifamily development. This is a retail consumer sentiment signal with indirect relevance: persistent inflation and consumer shift to discount formats suggest wage pressure and household budget constraints in middle-income demographics (back-to-school shoppers skew middle-class). This supports the case for workforce/middle-market multifamily in value-oriented submarkets over luxury trophy assets, but does not directly alter supply, demand, or capital for apartment development. Multifamily developers should note that strong dollar-store and mass-merchant expansion may correlate with secondary/tertiary market demographic inflow and labor-cost inflation in retail corridors—these are positive signals for workforce housing demand in markets like Memphis, Oklahoma City, and smaller Texas metros.

stories.title: CBRE Opens Hospitality-Inspired Office in Columbus
stories.excerpt: CBRE announced the opening of its new 19,500-square-foot office on the fifth floor at 10 W. Nationwide Blvd., a trophy office building within the Arena District owned and developed by Nationwide Realty Investors in downtown Columbus, Ohio. The firm has relocated from 200 Civic Center Drive. The new office is part of CBRE’s Workplace360 program, which showcases technology and a wide variety of collaborative spaces designed to support hybrid working. “As one of Columbus’s top trophy office buildings, 10 W. Nationwide Blvd. is an ideal home, and CBRE is proud to be its first tenant,” said CBRE’s Michael Copella, Executive Managing Director and Market Leader, Central Ohio. “The office design reflects the spirit of Columbus and its distinctive culture while highlighting the scope of CBRE’s capabilities.” New technology is installed throughout the office to support remote and in-person collaboration between employees. The office’s design reflects the unique character of Columbus, drawing inspiration from bespoke hotels and hospitality-focused experiences. The post CBRE Opens Hospitality-Inspired Office in Columbus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CBRE relocated to 10 W. Nationwide Blvd. (Columbus), 19,500 SF trophy office in downtown Arena District.
Nationwide Realty Investors owns/developed building; CBRE first major tenant in Class A Columbus CBD asset.
Office design emphasizes hybrid work support and hospitality-inspired collaboration; CBRE's Workplace360 program signal.
stories.summary: CBRE opened a new 19,500-square-foot office on the fifth floor of 10 W. Nationwide Boulevard in Columbus's downtown Arena District, relocating from 200 Civic Center Drive. The building is a trophy asset owned and developed by Nationwide Realty Investors. CBRE's space is designed under its Workplace360 program, which emphasizes technology, collaborative spaces, and hospitality-inspired design to support hybrid work arrangements. CBRE is the building's first major tenant.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily development. However, CBRE's commit to Columbus trophy office signals confidence in the metro's commercial real estate market. For multifamily developers: this is a *weak positive* for Columbus apartment demand—corporate tenants (CBRE, Nationwide, others) leasing Class A space indicate stable professional employment anchors. But the tenant roster is still thin; multifamily development should focus on workforce housing near employer clusters (Nationwide, insurance sector) rather than luxury downtown urban core. Secondary submarkets (New Albany, Delaware, Worthington) with suburban office parks and good transportation access to downtown remain stronger bets for rental demand.

stories.title: JLL Arranges Sale of Lincoln Park Retail Center to Curbline Properties
stories.excerpt: JLL has secured the sale of Clybourn Center, a 33,140-square-foot unanchored strip center strategically located in Chicago’s Lincoln Park neighborhood. JLL’s Capital Market Investment and Sales Advisory team, representing the seller, was led by Managing Director Michael Nieder and Director Brian Page. Leasing efforts at the center have been spearheaded by Steve Schwartz and his team. The buyer was Curbline Properties. Clybourn Center is strategically positioned at 2029-2073 N. Clybourn Ave., across from Foundry Park, a transformational $1 billion-plus mixed-use redevelopment project. When complete, Foundry Park will deliver approximately 3,700 residential units, 350,000 square feet of office space, 420,000 square feet of retail space, 250,000 square feet of hospitality space, and 12 acres of open space. The 91.2% occupied property features 86 surface-level parking spaces, which is a rare amenity for street-front retail in this dense urban corridor. Clybourn Center’s tenant mix is anchored by medical and wellness uses, with 38% of the gross leasable area occupied by medical tenants. The post JLL Arranges Sale of Lincoln Park Retail Center to Curbline Properties appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL brokered sale of Clybourn Center (Lincoln Park, Chicago, 33,140 SF retail) to Curbline Properties; 91.2% occupied.
Property anchored by medical/wellness tenants (38% of GLA); positioned near Foundry Park $1B+ mixed-use redevelopment.
Foundry Park will add 3,700 residential units, 350K SF office, 420K SF retail, 250K SF hospitality upon completion.
stories.summary: JLL facilitated the sale of Clybourn Center, a 33,140-square-foot unanchored retail strip in Chicago's Lincoln Park neighborhood, to Curbline Properties. The property is 91.2% occupied and features a tenant mix heavily anchored by medical and wellness uses (38% of GLA). It is positioned across from Foundry Park, a transformational $1 billion-plus mixed-use redevelopment that will deliver 3,700 residential units, 350,000 SF of office, 420,000 SF of retail, and 250,000 SF of hospitality with 12 acres of open space.
stories.impact: Moderate positive signal for Chicago multifamily. Foundry Park's 3,700 units represent a major supply anchor in Lincoln Park/Clybourn corridor; Curbline's acquisition of adjacent retail reflects confidence in the redevelopment's ability to drive foot traffic and tenant demand. For multifamily developers: the Foundry Park pipeline will absorb demand in this submarket through 2027–2029, muting rental growth in immediate vicinity. However, the project's density and mixed-use format will likely increase rents in secondary Lincoln Park neighborhoods (Bucktown, Ravenswood, surrounding blocks) as premium-priced units at Foundry Park absorb high-end demand. Focus development efforts on residential adjacent to Foundry Park but outside its master-plan footprint to capture spillover demand without competing directly on amenity intensity. Monitor retail performance at Clybourn Center post-acquisition; if Curbline upgrades and stabilizes it, a stronger retail ecosystem will support higher apartment rents nearby.

stories.title: MCA Realty Acquires 138K-SF Seattle-Area Industrial Portfolio
stories.excerpt: MCA Realty, Inc., a real estate investment company based in California, announced the acquisition of West Valley Distribution Center, a three-building 138,296-square-foot multi-tenant industrial portfolio located in Kent, Washington. The firm acquired the property from Tamarisk West Valley LLC with its MCA Realty Industrial Growth Fund II. MCA now owns three industrial properties encompassing 268,843 square feet in the Seattle MSA. Newmark’s Andrew Briner, Aaron Banks and Kevin Shannon represented both MCA Realty and the seller in the transaction. The firm’s Taylor Hoff and Thad Mallory have been retained to oversee leasing at the property.MCA Realty will make significant exterior and interior improvements. It also plans to reconfigure the suites. “This acquisition reflects our view that the Kent Valley will remain an attractive location for industrial occupants,” said Tyler Mattox, Principal at MCA Realty. “The property’s infill location, durable tenant demand, and upside potential make it a compelling addition to our portfolio.” Hear from the West Coast’s Leading Industrial Real Estate Experts | August 20Gain firsthand insight from the executives, investors, and developers navigating today’s industrial market. From shifting capital flows and debt trends to evolving occupier demand and development strategies, take part in the conversations shaping the future of industrial real estate. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post MCA Realty Acquires 138K-SF Seattle-Area Industrial Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: MCA Realty acquired West Valley Distribution Center (Kent, WA), 138K SF multi-tenant industrial portfolio, from Tamarisk.
MCA now owns 268.8K SF across three Seattle MSA industrial properties; plans exterior/interior upgrades and suite reconfiguration.
Kent Valley cited as attractive location for industrial occupants; MCA committing capital to value-add in infill market.
stories.summary: MCA Realty, a California-based real estate investment company, acquired West Valley Distribution Center in Kent, Washington—a three-building, 138,296-square-foot multi-tenant industrial portfolio—from Tamarisk West Valley LLC via its MCA Realty Industrial Growth Fund II. MCA now owns three industrial properties totaling 268,843 square feet in the Seattle MSA. The firm plans significant exterior and interior improvements and will reconfigure building suites. Newmark brokered the transaction and will oversee leasing.
stories.impact: Not relevant to multifamily development. This is an industrial logistics play in the Seattle submarket; no bearing on residential supply, demand, or financing dynamics.

stories.title: Rithm Capital Lands $515M Financing for Midtown Office Tower
stories.excerpt: Cushman & Wakefield and Newmark announced that their respective Equity, Debt & Structured Finance teams advised Rithm Capital in securing $515 million in fixed-rate financing for 31 W. 52nd St., a 29-story, 785,000-square-foot office tower in Midtown Manhattan. The Cushman & Wakefield team of Gideon Gil, Zach Kraft and Cecelia Galligan co-brokered the financing alongside Newmark’s Jordan Roeschlaub, Nick Scribani, Adam Spies, Adam Doneger, Tim Polglase, Dan Axelson and Jack Fenton. Led by Wells Fargo, the financing package consists of a $415-million senior mortgage, a $40 million B-Note and a $60-million mezzanine loan. The lending group also includes Bank of America, Barclays, Citi, Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan. The refinancing follows Rithm Capital’s acquisition of the larger Paramount Group office portfolio. “Securing financing of this scale from a consortium of leading lenders demonstrates continued confidence in well-positioned office assets in New York City’s premier business districts,” said Gil. “As capital markets continue to evolve, institutional lenders remain focused on high-quality properties backed by experienced ownership and strong long-term fundamentals.” The post Rithm Capital Lands $515M Financing for Midtown Office Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Rithm Capital secured $515M fixed-rate financing for 31 W. 52nd St. (Midtown Manhattan); 29-story, 785K SF office tower.
Wells Fargo lead; $415M senior + $40M B-note + $60M mezzanine. Consortium includes BofA, Barclays, Citi, Goldman, JPMorgan.
Refinance follows Rithm acquisition of larger Paramount Group office portfolio; trophy Manhattan office retains lender confidence.
stories.summary: Rithm Capital secured $515 million in fixed-rate financing for 31 W. 52nd Street, a 29-story, 785,000-square-foot office tower in Midtown Manhattan. The financing, led by Wells Fargo, comprises a $415 million senior mortgage, a $40 million B-Note, and a $60 million mezzanine loan, with co-lenders including Bank of America, Barclays, Citi, Goldman Sachs, and JPMorgan Chase. The refinancing follows Rithm's acquisition of Paramount Group's larger office portfolio and signals lender confidence in well-positioned Manhattan office assets.
stories.impact: Not relevant to multifamily development. This is a trophy office refinancing in Manhattan's core CBD. Indirect signal: institutional lenders remain willing to fund Manhattan office in Class A markets and experienced sponsorships (Rithm), suggesting liquidity at the top of the market—however, B-tier and secondary office buildings remain challenged. For multifamily developers in NYC: capital flowing to office assets may tighten debt availability for residential, especially in speculative or secondary markets. Focus on adaptive reuse (office-to-residential) in less-trophy neighborhoods (Astoria, Long Island City, Bushwick) where office conversion arbitrage is higher and regulatory support is present.

stories.title: Auto Parts Distributor Signs $42M Lease in Southern California
stories.excerpt: An auto parts distribution company is expanding in Southern California and has signed a lease to take over a 522,267-square-foot industrial building in the Inland Empire. The lease agreement with landlord Invesco is valued at more than $42 million, according to Daum Commercial Real Estate Services, which announced the deal Thursday and represented the tenant alongside Lara CRE. Daum said the lease represents a significant expansion of the tenant’s Southern California distribution footprint, but did not disclose the company’s name or the length of the lease. The property at 16380 Euclid Avenue is Building 3 at the Chino South Industrial Center in Chino, Calif. It was constructed in 2014 with 32-foot clear heights and dock-high loading bays. Chino’s direct industrial vacancy was at 3.24 percent in the first quarter with limited availability for large distribution space, according to Daum’s research. Daum’s Gus Andros represented the tenant along with Nathan Lara, Andrew Lara, Rudy Lara and Jack Nersesian of Lara CRE. Steve Bellitti, Tom Taylor, Joey Jones and Evelyn Manning of Colliers represented Invesco. Colliers did not immediately return a request for comment. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Auto parts distributor signed $42M lease on 522,267 SF industrial building (Chino, CA); Invesco landlord; Daum brokered.
Chino South Industrial Center (Building 3, 2014-built, 32-foot clear heights, dock-high bays); direct vacancy 3.24% Q1 2026.
Inland Empire industrial market tight; large distribution space availability limited.
stories.summary: An auto parts distributor (tenant name undisclosed) signed a lease for 522,267 square feet of industrial space at 16380 Euclid Avenue in Chino, California, at Chino South Industrial Center (Building 3). The lease value exceeds $42 million with landlord Invesco. The property, constructed in 2014, features 32-foot clear heights and dock-high loading bays. Chino's direct industrial vacancy was 3.24% in Q1 2026, with limited large-distribution-space availability.
stories.impact: Indirect positive signal for Southern California multifamily in Inland Empire. The Chino industrial market's 3.24% vacancy and limited large-space availability indicate strong logistics/distribution occupier demand and tight land supply. This drives labor in-migration (warehouse and distribution workers) into Inland Empire submarkets and pushes housing demand upward. For multifamily developers in Riverside County and San Bernardino County: monitor industrial absorption and vacancy rates as leading indicators of workforce housing demand. The auto parts/logistics sector is labor-intensive and typically attracts middle-income workers; workforce multifamily targeting $1,300–$1,800 rents in gateway communities (Temecula, Murrieta, Ontario, Victorville) near major industrial nodes will benefit from supply tightness in this sector. The 3.24% vacancy signals sustained industrial growth and justifies aggressive workforce housing development in secondary Inland Empire markets.

stories.title: D.C.’s Trophy Office Leasing Picks Up. The Rest, Not So Much.
stories.excerpt: Washington, D.C.’s office market recovery is becoming more concentrated within the cream of its crop. D.C. office leasing reached roughly 2 million square feet during the second quarter, the highest quarterly volume since 2024, according to Savills. Law firms alone leased about 600,000 square feet, and some large occupiers completed transactions, but the broader market remains hamstrung by empty office space and declining federal leasing. Office tenants are making long-term commitments in D.C. but they’re almost always choosing trophy assets, focusing on new or recently renovated buildings. Availability for D.C.’s Class A buildings was at 10 percent after the second quarter while the city’s overall availability rate increased to 24.2 percent. That increase comes despite 4 millions square feet of space coming off the market the past year from conversions. Similarly, overall asking rents increased 2.9 percent compared to the second quarter of 2025, while Class A rents rose more than 5 percent over the same period to $62.29 per square foot per year. Law firm White & Case signed the quarter’s largest lease in a move to nearly 196,000 square feet at 1701 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, while firms Steptoe, O’Melveny and Wilkinson Barker Knauer committed to long-term renewals. Tech companies also added to the uptick. Palantir renewed and expanded to 150,000 square feet in Georgetown, while Thomson Reuters signed for almost 33,000 square feet in the East End. Savills expects leasing momentum to carry into the second half of the year with deals nearing completion, including leases by Google and the U.S. Navy. Savills also said it expects office-to-residential conversion projects to continue to reduce inventory, particularly while the construction pipeline is low. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: D.C. Q2 2026 office leasing reached ~2M SF (highest quarterly since 2024); law firms leased 600K SF, large occupiers active.
Class A office availability 10% post-Q2; citywide availability 24.2% and rising despite 4M SF conversions off market.
Class A rents rose 5%+ YoY to $62.29/SF; overall rents up 2.9%. Trophy assets capture demand; secondary office distressed.
stories.summary: Washington, D.C.'s office market is experiencing a bifurcated recovery. Second-quarter 2026 leasing reached approximately 2 million square feet—the strongest quarterly volume since 2024—driven by law firms (600,000 SF) and large corporate occupiers. However, leasing is concentrated in Class A (new or recently renovated) assets, where availability stands at 10%. Class A rents rose over 5% year-over-year to $62.29 per square foot, while overall D.C. availability climbed to 24.2% despite 4 million square feet of conversions coming off the market over the past year. Declining federal leasing and empty secondary-market space continue to pressurize the broader market.
stories.impact: Weak signal for D.C. multifamily development. Office leasing strength is confined to trophy assets and legal/professional services anchors—not broad employment growth. The rising overall availability rate (24.2%) and persistent federal sector weakness suggest limited new job creation to support robust apartment demand. For developers: avoid speculative multifamily in downtown D.C. and focus on secondary markets with stronger employment anchors (Arlington, Reston, Bethesda) where technology and biotech corridors are driving job growth. The office-to-residential conversion pipeline (4M SF) may create demand for workforce housing, but capital for conversion will be scarce given financing challenges in the broader office market. The bifurcation toward Class A also signals that secondary office buildings (potential adaptive reuse candidates) will trade at significant discounts—an opportunity for patient capital and experienced conversion sponsors, but a headwind for speculative residential in office-heavy neighborhoods.

stories.title: Azorim Secures $69M Construction Loan for Yonkers Development
stories.excerpt: The fourth and final tower in a decade-long, multiphase development in Westchester County just secured construction capital. Israel-based development firm Azorim has secured $68.75 million in construction financing to build Miroza Tower 4, a 14-story, 174-unit luxury apartment tower that will complete the Miroza at Ridge Hill master-planned development in Yonkers, N.Y. Western Alliance Bank provided the debt, structured as a fixed-rate, interest-only construction loan, while Walker & Dunlop’s Jonathan Zilber, Joel Chetner and Mackenzie Kerin arranged the transaction. Zilber, senior managing director of capital markets real estate finance at Walker & Dunlop, described Azorim as “a best-in-class sponsor” in a statement, and noted that his firm previously arranged $145 million in new financing for the second and third towers of the Miroza development project for Azorim in late 2025. “Despite ongoing market headwinds, our deep familiarity with the sponsorship and ability to optimize the project’s PILOT and market-rate bond structure enabled us to deliver a highly competitive financing solution,” said Zilber. Miroza at Ridge Hill is at 599 Ridge Hill Boulevard in Yonkers, New York’s third-largest city after the Big Apple and Buffalo, and only 15 miles north of Manhattan on the Hudson River. Miroza Tower 4 will deliver 174 units and 248 parking spaces to Ridge Hill, a live-work-play town square near the Grassy Sprain Reservoir and Sprain Ridge Park in Yonkers. The four-building Miroza residential complex totals 520 units and saw the first three towers open in 2013, 2023 and 2024. Miroza’s buildings are connected by a 25,000-square-foot amenity center with a swimming pool, a basketball court, a movie theater and a running track. That connection turns the apartments into a self-contained city, and steps from the well-regarded restaurants and medical center of the Ridge Hill neighborhood. Azorim did not respond to requests for comment. Brian Pascus can be reached at
bullets: Azorim secured $68.75M construction loan (Western Alliance Bank) for Miroza Tower 4, Yonkers (14-story, 174-unit luxury).
Final phase of decade-long Miroza at Ridge Hill master-plan development in Westchester County. Walker & Dunlop arranged.
Azorim previously secured $145M construction financing for phases 2 & 3 in late 2025; lender confidence in sponsor despite headwinds.
stories.summary: Israel-based developer Azorim secured $68.75 million in fixed-rate, interest-only construction financing from Western Alliance Bank for Miroza Tower 4, a 14-story, 174-unit luxury apartment tower in Yonkers, New York. This final phase will complete the Miroza at Ridge Hill master-planned development in Westchester County. Walker & Dunlop arranged the financing. Azorim previously secured $145 million in construction financing for the second and third towers in late 2025, demonstrating sustained lender confidence despite broader market headwinds.
stories.impact: Positive supply and capital signal for Westchester County. Azorim's ability to secure back-to-back construction tranches ($145M in late 2025, $68.75M now) signals lender confidence in the sponsor and the Yonkers market fundamentals. The 174-unit final phase (cumulative 400+ units across all phases) represents meaningful new supply in the Ridge Hill master-plan submarket and will likely absorb demand from Manhattan and Bronx spillover. For Westchester developers: Azorim's execution demonstrates that construction capital flows to experienced sponsors with track records; Miroza's scale and phasing strategy de-risk financing. Secondary Westchester submarkets (New Rochelle, White Plains, Port Chester) with commuter rail access and emerging job centers (biotech, corporate back-offices) offer attractive development windows. Monitor Miroza's lease-up and rent trajectory (2028–2029 deliveries) to validate Westchester rent growth assumptions for competing projects.

stories.title: Prime Finance Refis Denver-Area Apartments With $46M Loan
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Oak Coast Properties and BMC Investments has sealed a $46.25 million loan to refinance a multifamily asset in suburban Denver, Commercial Observer has learned Prime Finance supplied the floating-rate loan on the 388-unit Aspen Park property in Northglenn, Colo. Walker & Dunlop arranged the transaction with a capital markets team consisting of Dustin Stolly, Justin Nelson, Aaron Appel, Jonathan Schwartz, Keith Kurland, Adam Schwartz, Sean Reimer, Michael Ianno, Eric Norris and Jason Schwartzberg. “Aspen Park is well positioned in one of metro Denver’s most connected suburban communities, with strong access to employment, transportation, and everyday amenities,” Stolly, senior managing director of capital markets institutional advisory at Walker & Dunlop, said in a statement. Located at 301 East Malley Drive 12 miles north of Downtown Denver, Aspen Park features one-, two- and three-bedroom garden-style apartments. Community amenities include a clubhouse, a fitness center, an indoor pool, a business center and a game room. Oak Coast Properties, BMC Investments and Prime Finance did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Prime Finance refinanced Aspen Park (Northglenn, CO, 388 units) with $46.25M floating-rate loan; Oak Coast/BMC JV borrower.
Northglenn location, 12 miles north of Downtown Denver, emphasizes suburban employment/transportation access.
Floating-rate refi at time of interest-rate uncertainty; refinancing market recovering for stabilized Colorado assets.
stories.summary: A joint venture between Oak Coast Properties and BMC Investments refinanced Aspen Park, a 388-unit garden-style apartment community in Northglenn, Colorado, with a $46.25 million floating-rate loan from Prime Finance. Walker & Dunlop arranged the transaction. The property, located at 301 East Malley Drive approximately 12 miles north of Downtown Denver, features one-, two-, and three-bedroom units and amenities including a clubhouse, fitness center, indoor pool, business center, and game room.
stories.impact: Neutral for Denver metro. This is a refinancing of a stabilized asset in suburban Denver—a positive signal for refinance capital availability, but limited insight into new development or demand growth. Northglenn's location on the northern corridor (I-25 corridor toward Fort Collins) suggests solid suburban workforce housing demand, but floating-rate debt exposure suggests the borrower is hedging on rate declines or planning a near-term exit. For developers: Denver refinancing remains accessible for stabilized assets with strong sponsors; new development financing is tighter. Focus on secondary Denver suburbs (Fort Collins, Boulder County, Evans) where population in-migration from Front Range tech employment is stronger and land availability is higher. The floating-rate refi also suggests the market anticipates rate normalization—monitor federal policy for signals on rate trajectory before locking long-term development debt.
